description: Drive a WLED LED strip from video content playing in Jellyfin
name: WLED TV
overview: Samples pixel colours along the edges of the playing video and sends them to a WLED controller in real time, creating an immersive edge-lighting effect that reacts to the content on screen.

versions.changelog:
## v1.3.1.0

The WebGL context rebuild in v1.3.0.0 did not resolve the post-resume slowdown, which rules out the context object as the cause. This release adds diagnostics to pinpoint the real bottleneck rather than guessing.

### Added
- **Debug mode** (renamed from the old "mock logging" toggle) — when enabled, shows a small on-screen overlay on the playing device with per-frame **capture time**, **send time**, **inter-frame gap**, and **WebSocket state/backlog**. This lets the frame-rate slowdown be diagnosed directly on the TV without a remote console.

### How to use
1. Enable **Debug mode** in the plugin settings.
2. Reproduce the slowdown (leave Jellyfin, return, play a video).
3. Read the overlay (top-left):
   - high `cap` → the GPU read-back is slow;
   - high `gap` with low `cap` → the browser is throttling the timer;
   - high `buf` / bad `ws` → the send path is stalled.

### Docs
- README now documents both **Debug mode** and **Update LEDs in batches**.

## v1.3.0.0

### Fixed
- **Edge lighting drops to ~1 fps after leaving and re-entering Jellyfin (WebOS)** — the WebGL capture context was created once and reused for the life of the page. Suspending/resuming the app on the TV leaves that context alive but degraded (correct colours, but ~1 fps), and it was never rebuilt. The context is now torn down and rebuilt on each playback start, and dropped when the app is backgrounded, so capture always runs on a fresh, full-speed context. Teardown releases the GPU context properly so repeated rebuilds cannot leak WebGL contexts.

This is why power-cycling the WLED controller had no effect — the bottleneck was the client-side GPU read-back, not the controller.

## v1.2.7.0

### Fixed
- **Subtitles hidden when edge lighting is active (WebOS)** — the aspect-ratio fix was changing the video element's `position`/`inset`, which altered its paint order and lifted it above Jellyfin's subtitle overlay. It now constrains only the box size (`width`/`height`/`margin`/`object-fit`) and never touches `position`, `inset`, or `z-index`. Size changes don't affect stacking order, so the subtitle layer renders on top exactly as in vanilla Jellyfin while the box still prevents the stretch.

### Known issue
- Frame rate on ESP8266 with a large strip remains limited by the controller's JSON throughput. Expected to be resolved by moving to an ESP32 (disable "Update LEDs in batches").

## v1.2.6.0

### Fixed
- **Subtitles hidden when edge lighting is active (WebOS)** — the aspect-ratio fix used `position: fixed`, which painted the video over Jellyfin's subtitle overlay. The letterbox box is now centred within the video's existing containing block, preserving the stacking order so subtitles show again. The stretch fix is unchanged.
- **Frame rate collapses to ~1 fps after the first video** — the old WebSocket buffer guard allowed a multi-frame backlog to accumulate that never drained once the ESP8266 fell behind. Added frame-level flow control: a frame is skipped if the previous one hasn't drained yet, capping latency at one frame and self-throttling to the controller's actual throughput. (This is also why changing the update interval previously had no effect.)

## v1.2.5.0

Reverts the v1.2.4.0 binary DRGB attempt (DRGB does not work over the WLED WebSocket) and replaces both fixes with root-cause approaches.

### Fixed
- **Final LED segment barely updates** — the WebSocket buffer guard drops whatever message is *last* in each per-frame burst when the ESP8266 falls behind, which with a fixed order is always the final segment. The batch send order is now **rotated every frame**, so any dropped update is distributed evenly across all segments instead of starving one.
- **Video stretched to 16:9 on LG TV (WebOS)** — `object-fit` is ignored by the WebOS media plane for hardware-decoded video (confirmed across three attempts). The video element's **box is now sized to the content's aspect ratio** and centred, so it physically cannot stretch; the player's black background forms the letterbox/pillarbox bars. Scoped to the WebGL capture path so desktop browsers are unaffected.

## v1.2.4.0

### Fixed
- **Final LED segment not updating at full frame rate** — switched batch mode from 5 × JSON messages to a single binary DRGB WebSocket frame (782 bytes for 260 LEDs). WLED applies the whole strip atomically in one main-loop pass; there are no longer inter-batch render gaps on ESP8266.
- **Video stretched to 16:9 on LG TV (WebOS)** — the video element is now promoted to a GPU compositing layer via `transform: translateZ(0)` on the first captured frame. This prevents WebOS from entering hardware-overlay mode so the overlay→composited transition that reset `object-fit` can no longer occur.
- **on/bri JSON sent every frame** — the `ledsOn` flag was never being set to `true` (trySend return value was discarded). Fixed so the brightness message is only sent once per WebSocket connection.

## v1.2.3.0

### Fixed
- **Final LED batch not updating** — replaced fixed 54-LED batches with a dynamic equal-size calculation. A 260-LED strip now uses 52-LED batches (5 × 52 = 260) instead of 4 × 54 + a boundary-touching remainder that WLED/ESP8266 mishandled.
- **Video stretched to 16:9 on LG TV (WebOS)** — moved `object-fit: contain` enforcement to *after* the canvas/WebGL frame read, using `setProperty` with `!important`. On WebOS, `texImage2D(video)` can synchronously reset the video element's `object-fit`; the previous fix applied it too early.

Fix video displayed stretched when plugin samples frames. On platforms where ctx.drawImage() pulls video out of hardware-overlay mode (WebOS, Tizen, some browsers), the video element's inline object-fit was being reset to the browser default (fill = stretch). The plugin now explicitly ensures object-fit:contain on every tick.

Fix last LED batch ignored by WLED when strip length is not a multiple of 54. The final partial batch is now padded with black (000000) to match the size of all preceding batches. LED indices beyond the configured strip length are ignored by WLED, so padding is safe.

Add 'Update LEDs in batches' setting. When enabled (default, ESP8266 compatible), colours are sent in 54-LED batches. When disabled, all LEDs are sent in a single frame — intended for ESP32 and other controllers with ample heap.
Raise mostly-black frame threshold from 5% to 6.5% to suppress flickering on denser credit frames.
Skip letterbox/pillarbox crop on mostly-black frames to prevent credits from blasting white LEDs.

Fix mock-logging checkbox not rendering (emby-checkbox polyfill skipped it).

Device dropdown now filtered: hides Jellyseerr entries, devices inactive > 2 weeks, and devices last used by login-screen-hidden users.

WebGL: replace ctx.drawImage cross-context copy with gl.readPixels — eliminates the GPU pipeline flush causing ~800 ms per frame on WebOS.

Fixes mock-logging checkbox not rendering in settings.

Performance: 1/4-scale capture and single getImageData call per frame.

Reduces WebGL GPU readback from ~6 MB to ~400 KB; eliminates per-region getImageData calls. Expected to bring refresh rate from ~800 ms to ~50-100 ms on WebOS.

Also fixes the mock-logging checkbox not rendering in the settings page.

Add WebGL capture method setting and mock debug logging toggle.

- New 'Video capture method' setting: Canvas 2D (default) or WebGL for hardware-overlay platforms like WebOS where Canvas 2D returns black pixels.
- New 'Send debug logs to mock WLED server' checkbox, off by default.

Fix: LEDs silent on WebOS/hardware-overlay platforms.

New setting: Video capture method. Select WebGL if the LEDs do not turn on during playback (hardware overlay issue on WebOS/smart TV platforms).
New setting: Send debug logs to mock WLED server (keeps mock log clean by default).
Add plugin icon (shown in Jellyfin's plugin catalogue).

First stable release.

Full-featured WLED edge-lighting plugin for Jellyfin:
- Direct browser → WLED WebSocket connection
- Auto-detects letterbox/pillarbox bars; samples only the actual picture area
- Per-device restriction: limit edge lighting to one specific Jellyfin client
- Inline connection test against the live URL input value
- Clockwise/counter-clockwise strip direction, configurable start position
- Adjustable brightness, sample depth, and update rate
